Everything is too loud
Overall gain or MPO too high, acclimatization difficulty, hyperacusis, incorrect fitting formula, poor loudness balancing, recruitment, input-specific mismatch.
Check otoscopy, device function, datalogging, real-ear output for soft/average/loud/MPO, loudness discomfort, recent hearing change, and patient examples.

General: lower overall gain or MPO and verify comfort. Specific: reduce high-level gain and/or MPO in the affected bands, often 2000-6000 Hz or 4000-8000 Hz if sharp sounds are painful; consider reducing mid-input gain only if average speech is too loud; consider acclimatization manager with a scheduled increase.
Increase venting if occlusion contributes; review receiver power, dome/mold coupling, insertion depth, and wax filter.
Comfort is needed for daily use, but too much reduction can reduce speech access. Make small verified changes and track the exact sound examples.
Pain, sudden change, hyperacusis with severe distress, dizziness, drainage, asymmetric symptoms.
ENT/medical for red flags; mental health or tinnitus/sound tolerance specialist for severe distress; manufacturer only for device fault.
Adjusted loudness based on patient report and verification; reduced output in targeted frequency/input region; counseled on audibility versus comfort and follow-up plan.
Speech is unclear

Insufficient high-frequency audibility, poor speech-in-noise ability, blocked receiver, inappropriate venting, frequency lowering mismatch, cognitive/listening fatigue, auditory deprivation, poor device use.
Check audiogram update, speech testing, QuickSIN/BKB-SIN when appropriate, real-ear match, wax filter, microphone ports, dome/mold seal, datalogging, and patient goal.

General: improve audibility while preserving comfort. Specific: increase soft and average input gain for 2000-6000 Hz if under target; review frequency lowering start/cutoff and strength; avoid overboosting high frequencies if distortion or feedback emerges; verify aided audibility.
Consider more closed coupling if feedback limits high-frequency gain; replace clogged parts; consider remote microphone for noise/distance.
Loudness is not the same as clarity. Noise, distance, and brain listening load still matter.
Sudden clarity decline, asymmetric speech discrimination, unilateral tinnitus, neurologic symptoms.
ENT/medical for red flags; CI referral if limited aided benefit despite appropriate amplification.
Reviewed clarity complaint; verified device function and audibility; adjusted high-frequency average/soft gain and counseled on speech-in-noise limitations.
Background noise is overwhelming
Omnidirectional program, too much low-frequency gain, poor directional mic activation, weak noise management, unrealistic expectations, poor SNR, cognitive fatigue.
Reproduce complaint; check program use, directionality, noise reduction, datalogging, venting, REM, QuickSIN, communication strategies.

General: optimize noise program and directional microphones. Specific: reduce low-frequency gain for soft inputs if ambient hum is intrusive; increase noise reduction in dedicated noise program; set directional mic mode more aggressively for restaurants; reduce 125-750 Hz gain only if speech access remains acceptable.
Consider remote microphone, seating strategies, coupling that supports directional benefits, clean microphone ports.
Hearing aids improve access but do not erase noise. Teach patient to face talker and use remote mic when distance/noise is high.
Sudden change, unilateral symptoms, severe dizziness, neurologic concerns.
Refer for medical red flags; consider assistive technology evaluation or auditory rehab.
Created/modified noise program; reviewed SNR strategies; discussed remote microphone and realistic expectations.
My own voice is boomy
Occlusion effect, closed dome/mold, excessive low-frequency gain, deep insertion issue, vent blocked, own-voice processing mismatch.
Check physical fit, vent patency, insertion depth, unaided low-frequency hearing, REM, patient voice sample.

General: reduce occlusion and low-frequency excess. Specific: increase venting or use more open coupling if appropriate; reduce low-frequency gain for own-voice region, often 125-750 Hz, especially for soft and average inputs; avoid reducing speech-critical audibility unnecessarily.
Modify vent, change dome, remake mold, adjust canal length or receiver placement.
Own voice often improves with adaptation, but severe occlusion usually needs acoustic change, not only counseling.
Pain, drainage, ear canal irritation, sudden change.
ENT for ear symptoms; lab/remake for acoustic fit issue.
Addressed own-voice complaint; vent/coupling reviewed; low-frequency gain adjusted and patient voice checked in office.
Whistling or feedback
Poor seal, loose earmold, cerumen, cracked tubing, receiver migration, high-frequency gain too high, venting mismatch, feedback manager not calibrated.
Otoscopy, physical fit, feedback test, receiver/dome/mold inspection, real-ear, hand near ear test, jaw movement test.

General: run feedback management and review gain. Specific: reduce only the narrow high-frequency region causing feedback if necessary; avoid broad high-frequency cuts that harm speech; review frequency lowering if feedback limits 4000-8000 Hz audibility.
Replace dome/filter/tubing, remake mold, lengthen canal lock, change receiver strength, reduce vent if medically and acoustically appropriate.
Feedback is usually fixable, but the cause must be identified before gain is removed.
Cerumen impaction, pain, drainage, abnormal otoscopy, sudden hearing change.
ENT for cerumen or ear disease; manufacturer/lab for repair or remake.
Feedback assessed; otoscopy and physical fit reviewed; feedback manager completed; acoustic modification discussed.
Sharp sounds hurt or S sounds are too sharp
Excess high-frequency gain or output, MPO too high, frequency lowering artifacts, sound tolerance issue, recruitment, hyperacusis.
Identify trigger sounds; check REM for loud/MPO inputs; review frequency lowering, compression, acoustic fit, and loudness discomfort.

General: reduce sharpness while maintaining consonant access. Specific: reduce high-level gain or MPO around 3000-8000 Hz; reduce average input gain 4000-8000 Hz if average speech is sharp; adjust frequency lowering strength/cutoff if /s/ is distorted; consider separate comfort program.
Consider receiver strength, dome/mold acoustics, venting, and physical discomfort.
Reduce pain first, then rebuild audibility. Warn that too much high-frequency reduction can reduce speech clarity.
Pain, sudden onset sound intolerance, dizziness, neurologic signs, severe distress.
ENT/medical or sound-tolerance specialist when red flags or severe intolerance are present.
Adjusted high-frequency output for reported sharpness; verified comfort and counseled on speech audibility tradeoffs.
Women or children sound too shrill
Too much high-frequency gain, frequency lowering setting too strong, receiver/mold resonance, acclimatization issue.
Use live voice examples; check REM, frequency lowering, compression, and patient sound samples.

General: soften high-frequency emphasis. Specific: reduce average input gain at 3000-6000 Hz or 4000-8000 Hz in small steps; reduce frequency lowering strength or raise start frequency if high-pitched voices sound unnatural; check MPO if voices are painful.
Review dome/mold, receiver, insertion, and venting.
Explain that high-frequency audibility helps speech clarity but should not be painfully sharp.
Sudden new distortion, unilateral change, neurologic symptoms.
Medical referral for red flags; manufacturer repair if sound remains distorted with correct programming.
Fine-tuned high-frequency average gain/frequency lowering based on patient voice examples.
I cannot hear soft speech
Under-amplification for soft inputs, conservative adaptation, open fitting leakage, blocked receiver, insufficient high-frequency audibility, dead region concerns.
Check REM for soft speech, SII/audibility estimates, device condition, battery/charge, coupling, patient environments.

General: increase soft speech audibility. Specific: increase low-level gain for 1000-6000 Hz where below target; avoid raising MPO unless loud sounds are also insufficient; review expansion if soft speech is being suppressed.
More closed coupling if needed; clean/replace receiver, filters, dome, or mold.
Soft speech may improve with verified gain, but distance and noise still matter.
Sudden threshold change, asymmetric symptoms, ear disease signs.
Medical referral for red flags; CI evaluation when aided speech access remains poor despite optimized fitting.
Verified soft-speech output; increased soft-input gain in targeted bands and reviewed communication distance.
Restaurants are impossible
Poor SNR, insufficient directional or remote microphone use, reverberation, unrealistic expectations, poor binaural integration, cognitive fatigue.
Assess QuickSIN/functional report, programs, remote mic availability, binaural fitting, datalogging, goals.

General: optimize dedicated restaurant program. Specific: directional microphone focus, stronger noise reduction, modest low-frequency soft-input reduction for environmental noise, maintain mid/high average-input speech audibility, add remote mic setting.
Consider remote mic, binaural fit, clean microphones, coupling.
Teach seating, lighting, one talker at a time, and remote mic. Devices cannot remove all competing speech.
Sudden change or neurologic symptoms.
Aural rehab, assistive tech, medical referral for red flags.
Reviewed restaurant complaint; adjusted noise program and counseled on SNR and remote microphone options.
Wind noise is terrible
Microphone wind turbulence, outdoor program not active, excessive low-frequency gain, microphone cover blockage.
Check microphone ports, program settings, outdoor examples, physical placement.

General: enable or strengthen wind management. Specific: reduce low-frequency gain below 750 Hz in outdoor program if wind rumble dominates; use directional/wind algorithm options; preserve speech frequencies when possible.
Clean mic ports, consider retention/hat interaction, device placement, remote mic location.
Wind is a microphone physics problem, not patient failure. Outdoor programs may trade some clarity for comfort.
None specific unless sudden hearing symptoms or dizziness.
Manufacturer repair if microphones malfunction; medical referral for red flags.
Outdoor wind complaint addressed; wind management adjusted and low-frequency outdoor gain reviewed.
Music sounds distorted
Compression, frequency lowering, noise reduction, feedback manager, insufficient bandwidth, too much high-frequency gain, input limiting.
Ask music type and listening mode; test music program; review streaming versus acoustic input; check REM and MPO.

General: create a music program with fewer speech-processing artifacts. Specific: disable or reduce frequency lowering for music if appropriate; reduce aggressive noise reduction; adjust MPO/input limiting; reduce narrow high-frequency peaks causing harshness; use slower compression if available.
Check coupling, receiver headroom, streaming path, Bluetooth codec limitations.
Music may require a separate program because speech settings are optimized for speech, not fidelity.
Sudden distortion in one ear or new neurologic/vestibular symptoms.
Manufacturer support for hardware/streaming faults; medical referral for red flags.
Music program adjusted with reduced processing artifacts; counseled on music versus speech goals.
Phone calls or streaming are poor
Bluetooth pairing, streaming balance, phone microphone, program routing, weak battery, app setting, poor cellular connection, one-ear streaming.
Check pairing, app, phone settings, streaming route, microphones, battery/charge, direct acoustic phone option.

General: confirm routing and streaming gain. Specific: increase streaming input gain or streaming balance if too soft; reduce environmental microphone during streaming if noise competes; verify phone program gain around speech frequencies 1000-4000 Hz.
Clean microphones, update firmware through official channels, confirm phone compatibility using official manufacturer pages.
Not all phone problems are hearing aid problems. Phone model, signal, app, and streaming route matter.
Sudden hearing changes, pain, dizziness.
Manufacturer support for device-specific pairing or compatibility; medical referral for red flags.
Streaming/phone complaint reviewed; routing and gain balance checked; official support link provided.
Sound cuts in and out
Moisture, receiver wire intermittency, battery/charger failure, loose dome/mold, Bluetooth instability, clogged microphones, software issue.
Listen-check, battery/charger test, physical inspection, moisture check, receiver swap, datalogging, streaming versus acoustic distinction.

General: separate acoustic intermittency from streaming intermittency. Specific: do not compensate with gain until hardware is stable; if acoustic output is stable but streaming drops, review streaming settings and phone compatibility.
Replace receiver/wire/dome/filter; dry device; repair if intermittent in test box.
Intermittency is often hardware or connectivity, not the patient adapting poorly.
Pain, drainage, sudden threshold change, dizziness.
Manufacturer repair/support for hardware or Bluetooth; medical referral for red flags.
Intermittency evaluated; hardware/connectivity checks completed; repair/support plan documented.
